metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the fail-open/fail-closed split in the generalized DET consumer: specify whether the consumer switches behavior by criterion namespace (e.g., `project.*` → fail-open, `rebar.builtin.security.*` → fail-closed) or introduces a per-criterion `fail_mode` routing field, so the 'Generalize the DET-scan consumer' step has an unambiguous behavioral spec.
findings.finding: The 'DET-invariant scan scoping' section mandates fail-open for project DET invariants ('engine binary absent / parse-error / timeout → abstain → skipped = PASS, never FAIL'), but the existing `code_review/detectors.py` consumer uses `apply_failclosed` (explicitly noted in 'What exists'). The 'Net-new' section says to generalize this consumer for project invariants without specifying that the generalized path must switch to fail-open behavior while the existing built-in security path retains fail-closed — leaving implementers with two contradictory behavioral specs for the same code path.
findings.location: 'DET-invariant scan scoping & fail-open' section vs. 'What exists vs. what we build' section (apply_failclosed callout) vs. 'Net-new' (Generalize the DET-scan consumer)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `fail_mode: open|closed` field to the criterion routing record (defaulting to `open` for project criteria, `closed` for built-in security); document this in the overlay schema section and thread it through the generalized consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism by which activating a project LLM criterion adds its `include_project.<name>` slot to the `plan-review.yaml` batch fan-out — options include: (a) a regeneration step analogous to the `reviewers/index.json` drift gate that appends new batch entries to the YAML, (b) a runtime-dynamic batch-membership extension in the workflow engine, or (c) an explicit per-project YAML overlay file that extends the packaged `plan-review.yaml`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that project criteria will fan out through the Pass-1 batch vocabulary by routing through `effective_criteria(repo_root)` and threading `workflow_ops`, but the actual fan-out mechanism is a statically-authored YAML batch list where every criterion has a hardcoded `when: '${{ steps.assemble.outputs.include_<ID> }}'` entry — a new `project.my_rule` criterion would need a corresponding YAML entry that no existing codepath generates.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'Route it through every vocabulary site: … workflow Pass-1 batch vocabulary (`workflow_ops` builds the `include_<ID>` fan-out from the canonical set at workflow-definition time — project ids must enter it too, else an activated criterion never fans out)'; also § Acceptance Criteria AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-blast-radius option consistent with the plan's 'no new DSL' posture: extend `plan_review_assemble_criteria` to emit `include_<project_id>` outputs for activated project criteria AND add a 'CI routing derivation/validation pipeline' step (already mentioned in the last AC) that regenerates the `plan-review.yaml` batch section from `effective_criteria(repo_root)` and diffs it — mirroring the `reviewers/index.json` regenerate+diff gate already cited in the plan. This keeps the YAML the source of truth while making it derivable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `project.<name>`-prefixed criterion ids map to valid `include_<ID>` workflow output keys (e.g. sanitize `.` → `_` to produce `include_project_my_criterion`, and document that the workflow expression uses the sanitized form) so activated project criteria actually fan out into the Pass-1 batch.
findings.finding: Project criterion ids use the `project.<name>` namespace prefix, but `plan_review_assemble_criteria` (workflow_ops.py:165) emits output keys as `f"include_{cid}"` — making the workflow output key for a project criterion `include_project.foo` (containing a `.`). This is not a valid identifier in the `${{ steps.assemble.outputs.include_project.foo }}` workflow expression syntax (`.` is a path-separator), so an activated project criterion would never fan out into the Pass-1 batch, silently skipping it regardless of activation.
findings.location: § Discovery — `effective_criteria(repo_root)`; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:165` (`out: dict[str, Any] = {f"include_{cid}": (cid in included) for cid in canonical}`); AC: 'project ids must enter it too, else an activated criterion never fans out'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a key-sanitization rule: `include_{cid.replace('.', '_')}` (or similar), applied consistently in `plan_review_assemble_criteria` when building output keys AND in the workflow YAML when writing `when:` expressions. Document this sanitization as part of the overlay schema contract so workflow YAML authors use the sanitized form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define whether `POST /criterion/preview` returns the existing `review_result` shape (so `_recall`/`_no_fire` scorers apply unchanged) or a new `{verdict: "fire"|"no-fire"}` shape, and if the latter, specify how the per-criterion eval runner maps its output to the existing `FIRE_EXPECTS`/`NOFIRE_EXPECTS` scorer vocabulary (or define a new scorer that handles the new shape).
findings.finding: `POST /criterion/preview` introduces a new `{verdict: "fire"|"no-fire"}` output enum whose value vocabulary is never reconciled with the existing eval-fixture `expect` vocabulary (`FIRE_EXPECTS={"finding","fail"}`, `NOFIRE_EXPECTS={"pass"}` in `eval_scorers.py:32-33`). The plan says the preview "reuses the per-criterion eval runner" but if the runner returns `fire`/`no-fire` rather than a `review_result` with findings, the existing `_recall`/`_no_fire` scorers will produce `applicable=False` on every fixture case, silently making the calibration metrics vacuous.
findings.location: § Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview; AC line: `POST /criterion/preview` (`{criterion, fixture} → {verdict, finding?, rationale}`); `src/rebar/llm/eval_scorers.py:32-33` (`FIRE_EXPECTS`, `NOFIRE_EXPECTS`)
findings.suggested_fix: Have the per-criterion eval runner return a `review_result`-shaped output (findings list) rather than a bare `fire`/`no-fire` enum, so the existing `_recall`/`_no_fire` scorers apply without modification. The `fire`/`no-fire` display label in the preview UI is then a presentation-layer derivation (`fired = bool(findings)`), not a new wire schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `drift_refresh_candidate` and `remediation_mode_candidate` to the Migration callsite list and add an AC sub-bullet (or extend the registry_version AC) verifying that both callers pass `repo_root` so overlay changes invalidate all affected attestation checks.
findings.finding: The 'Attestation & invalidation' section explicitly lists `drift_refresh_candidate` and `remediation_mode_candidate` as callers that must receive `repo_root` threading ('which call registry_version() today without it'), but the 'Migration & compatibility' section's callsite list omits both, and no AC verifies that these two callers are updated. This creates a cross-section gap: the attestation section declares a required change that the migration plan and ACs don't track.
findings.location: 'Attestation & invalidation' section vs. 'Migration & compatibility' Callsites bullet vs. Acceptance Criteria (registry_version AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the async fallback shape for `POST /criterion/preview` (at minimum: response schema for job-id, poll endpoint path + response contract, timeout/cancellation behavior) and define what 'reference model' means for the spike's pass/fail threshold, so the executor can implement either branch without stopping to ask.
findings.finding: The `POST /criterion/preview` spike gate defers a real architectural decision to the executor without specifying the async fallback design. The plan says 'if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout on the reference model, fall back to an async job+poll shape' but provides no design for the async shape (endpoint structure, job-id response, poll URL, poll interval, cancellation, error states). If the spike triggers the fallback path, the executor has nothing to implement against.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — Editor live preview bullet; AC: 'The visual editor authors a project criterion … A spike first validates sync-execution feasibility.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection: 'Async fallback (if spike fails): `POST /criterion/preview` returns `{job_id: str, poll_url: str}` immediately; `GET /criterion/preview/{job_id}` returns `{status: pending|done|error, result?: {verdict, finding?, rationale}}`. Timeout = 60s wall-clock from POST. Reference model = the gate's currently configured default LLM (from `rebar.llm.config`). Spike pass criterion: p95 latency < 45s on 5 consecutive fixture calls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the spike's pass/fail threshold (e.g., 'p95 < 60 s on claude-sonnet-4 with a minimal must-fire fixture'), specify the timeout-enforcement mechanism for blocking LLM calls inside the `ThreadingHTTPServer` handler thread (e.g., `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` with `Future.result(timeout=…)`), and sketch the async fallback's endpoint contract (poll URL, job-id shape) so the architectural branch can be decided before build starts.
findings.finding: The `POST /criterion/preview` spike gate names a fallback (async job+poll) but provides no pass/fail threshold — what latency, what timeout value, what test scenario determines 'sync feasible'? Without a defined exit criterion the spike is open-ended and the architectural branch (sync vs. async) cannot be decided before committing the full plan.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' paragraph; AC line: 'A spike first validates sync-execution feasibility'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the async fallback response contract: what HTTP status and body `POST /criterion/preview` returns when dispatching async (e.g. HTTP 202 `{job_id: str}`), the poll endpoint path and response shape, and whether this implies a new editor endpoint is added to the allow-list in `do_POST`.
findings.finding: The `POST /criterion/preview` 'spike gate' fallback is structurally incomplete: the plan says 'if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout on the reference model, fall back to an async job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's `ThreadingHTTPServer` handler' — but neither the async response shape, the poll endpoint, nor the HTTP status code for the fallback path are defined. The `ThreadingHTTPServer` handler must return synchronously; the async fallback implies a new `GET /criterion/preview/status?job_id=X` endpoint (or similar) that does not exist in the plan's POST set.
findings.location: § Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview (spike gate bullet); `src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor.py:613` (existing POST set: `/save`, `/prompt/save`, `/validate`, `/library/create`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Carve Build-order steps 3 (eval runner + calibration view) and 4 (editor live-preview) into formal child tickets with their own AC sets, so the core registry MVP (steps 1–2) can ship and be attested independently.
findings.finding: The epic carries 13 ACs spanning at least five independent concerns (unified registry/discovery, DET generalization, eval runner + calibration view, visual editor live-preview, attestation port + docs), but decomposes only into a sequenced build-order list rather than formal child tickets with their own ACs. Steps 3 (eval runner / calibration view) and 4 (editor live-preview) are independently deliverable and can fail/be descoped without affecting the core registry MVP — yet they share a single AC scope, making prioritization and partial delivery ambiguous.
findings.location: Build order (risk-ordered MVP) § steps 3–4; Acceptance Criteria (13 ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Create two child tickets: (a) 'Project-criteria eval runner + calibration view' (ACs covering `rebar criteria eval`, recall/κ metrics, fixture harness); (b) 'Visual editor live-preview for project criteria' (ACs covering `POST /criterion/preview`, spike gate, timeout). Link both as follow-ons to the registry MVP ticket. The parent epic retains the architecture narrative and the registry/DET/attestation ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the `rebar.llm.criteria` AC into two clearly separated items: (1) an in-session deliverable ('`rebar.llm.criteria` module exists and plan-review consumes it exclusively — code-review consumers still use per-gate registries') and (2) a post-b744 child AC ('code-review adopts `rebar.llm.criteria`; `review_result` shape preserved'), so each is independently evaluable.
findings.finding: The shared `rebar.llm.criteria` AC is simultaneously in-scope (plan-review part now) and out-of-scope (code-review adoption post-b744), but the AC text does not delineate what observable outcome is due in this session vs. deferred. An agent closing this epic cannot determine whether the AC is 'done' if b744 hasn't landed.
findings.location: AC: 'A shared `rebar.llm.criteria` layer that both plan-review and code-review consume, preserving `review_code`'s `review_result` shape. Gated on b744…'

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that if `effective_routing` has any in-process cache (mtime-keyed or otherwise), `_invalidate_caches()` must also clear it; add this to the implementation spec to prevent the editor authoring flow from serving stale routing after a `/library/create` call.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'extend `prompt_library._invalidate_caches()`' but does not state that `effective_routing` itself needs a cache-clear path. If `effective_routing` is implemented with any form of caching (mtime-keyed or otherwise), the existing `_invalidate_caches()` (which clears only `load_criteria` and `_routing_index`) must also clear it — otherwise the editor's `/library/create` authoring flow (which calls `_invalidate_caches()` after writing a new prompt) will leave a stale `effective_routing` cache serving the old criterion set. This is an unspecified ordering dependency in the invalidation chain.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'extend `prompt_library._invalidate_caches()` (already clears `load_criteria`/`_routing_index`)'; src/rebar/llm/prompt_library.py:230-237
findings.suggested_fix: If `effective_routing` is uncached (preferred per the prior finding), this issue disappears. If any caching is added, add `registry.effective_routing.cache_clear()` (or equivalent) to `_invalidate_caches()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the collision/rejection error contract with the overlay schema definition: specify what a 'located error' produces (e.g. a `RegistryError` with `{gate, criterion_id, conflict_type}` fields, or a structured JSON error the CI gate parses) so the load-time validation AC is unambiguously implementable.
findings.finding: The overlay collision/rebind rejection rules (un-prefixed built-in id → re-tune/disable; `project.`-prefixed id equal to a built-in → rejected at load; duplicate project id → rejected) are stated in the narrative § Overlay schema section but are NOT co-located with any error-message contract, making it impossible for an implementer to know what a 'located error' should contain (which id, which gate, which conflict type). The plan says 'rejected at load (a located error)' but never specifies the error shape/message format that a downstream CI drift gate or a human operator would parse.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'Namespace / collision (load-time, located error)' bullet; AC line: 'load-time collision/rebind rejection (a located error)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the routing derivation/validation pipeline (load-time validation + CI drift/parity gate) as an explicit numbered step in the Build Order, or assign it to an existing step (e.g., step 1 or 2) with a rationale for where it fits in the risk-ordered sequence.
findings.finding: The 'Net-new' deliverables list includes 'Routing derivation/validation pipeline (criteria_routing.json is hand-maintained; no generator/drift gate)' and it has a corresponding AC, but the 'Build Order' section (5 numbered steps) has no step that covers this deliverable — it is absent from the risk-ordered MVP sequence entirely.
findings.location: 'Net-new (build)' section (last bullet) vs. 'Build Order (risk-ordered MVP)' section (steps 1–5) vs. AC 'A routing derivation/validation pipeline...'
findings.suggested_fix: Insert as step 2a or fold into step 1: 'Add load-time routing validation (located error on invalid overlay entry) as part of the overlay activation machinery; add the CI drift/parity gate alongside the DET generalization (step 2) since both depend on the overlay schema being stable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `POST /criterion/preview` endpoint contract (request/response schema, timeout behavior, and the sync-vs-async fallback decision) as an explicit deliverable in the docs/ADR section — and note that the spike outcome must be reflected in the final docs before the AC is closed.
findings.finding: The `POST /criterion/preview` endpoint introduces a new HTTP API contract (`{criterion_id | inline(prompt+routing), fixture} → {verdict, finding?, rationale}`, configurable timeout, async fallback shape) but the docs deliverable does not explicitly include documenting this endpoint's contract — leaving tooling authors and CI integrators without a specified reference.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — Editor live-preview bullet; Docs deliverable (Migration & compatibility); AC: visual-editor preview AC
findings.suggested_fix: In the Docs deliverable bullet, append: '…and the `POST /criterion/preview` HTTP contract (input schema, response shape, timeout/async-fallback behavior — updated once the spike gate resolves the sync-vs-async shape).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the spike-gate framing for `POST /criterion/preview`: `ThreadingHTTPServer` spawns one thread per request so a slow handler does NOT block other requests; the actual risk is thread exhaustion under concurrent preview submissions; the spike should validate concurrent-request behavior (e.g., 3 simultaneous 60s LLM calls) and cap the thread count or bound the concurrent-preview queue if needed.
findings.finding: The plan's spike-gate rationale for `POST /criterion/preview` states it should 'fall back to an async job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's `ThreadingHTTPServer` handler.' But `http.server.ThreadingHTTPServer` spawns a new OS thread per request (confirmed at editor.py:759); a slow LLM handler therefore does NOT block other requests — each request gets its own thread. The actual failure mode the plan should guard against is thread exhaustion (many concurrent 60-second LLM calls accumulating threads) and memory pressure, not single-handler blocking. The current framing may lead the implementer to design the spike incorrectly (e.g., treating a 200ms response time as the threshold when the real concern is concurrent-request fan-out).
findings.location: § Net-new — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' — 'Spike gate: if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout on the reference model, fall back to an async job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's `ThreadingHTTPServer` handler'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `effective_routing` cache-keying ambiguity: prefer the uncached variant (read the overlay file fresh each call — it is a small JSON file and the call frequency is low) over the mtime-keyed variant, which introduces a TOCTOU race between mtime-read and file-read in the long-lived MCP server; document the rationale in the implementation.
findings.finding: The proposed `effective_routing(repo_root)` cache keyed by `(repo_root, overlay mtime)` has a TOCTOU race: the mtime is read to form the cache key, but the file content is read separately afterward. In a long-lived MCP server (the scenario the plan explicitly targets), a concurrent overlay edit between mtime-read and file-read will cache stale content under the new mtime key, defeating the isolation guarantee. The plan presents 'uncached, or keyed by (repo_root, overlay mtime)' as equivalent options, but they are not: the mtime-keyed variant introduces a hidden ordering assumption.
findings.location: § Overlay schema, activation & caching — 'add `effective_routing(repo_root)` (uncached, or keyed by `(repo_root, overlay mtime)`)'; also § Attestation AC 'A RED test must prove no cross-repo routing leakage'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the mtime-keyed option and specify `effective_routing(repo_root)` as unconditionally uncached (no `@lru_cache`). The packaged `_routing_index()` stays `@lru_cache(maxsize=1)` (immutable per binary). Reads of a small JSON overlay file are cheap; the correctness simplification is worth the minor per-call cost.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
